label: Energy Intensity per Rupee of Turnover

sections.about: This metric measures total energy consumed per rupee of turnover under BRSR Principle 6, Essential Indicator 1. It links energy load with revenue productivity, but is affected by product mix, steel prices, downstream value addition and unit normalisation. A lower value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: Jindal Stainless leads in all three years: 0.0000869 GJ/₹ (869 GJ/Crore INR) in FY2023, improving to 0.0000916 GJ/₹ in FY2024 and 0.0000798 GJ/₹ in FY2025, supported by high-value stainless product mix and lower absolute energy base. Tata worsened from 0.00032 GJ/₹ in FY2023 to 0.00040 GJ/₹ in FY2024 and 0.00044 GJ/₹ in FY2025. JSW also worsened from 0.00038 to 0.000421 GJ/₹. SAIL held steady at 0.00057 GJ/₹ in FY2023 and FY2024, improving marginally to 0.000527 GJ/₹ in FY2025.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2025, Tata ranks 3rd, with energy intensity per rupee 451.7% above Jindal Stainless. Tata’s rank was 2nd in FY2023, declined to 3rd in FY2024 and stayed 3rd in FY2025. The decline reflects both higher total energy per unit revenue and the structural advantage Jindal gets from stainless value realisation combined with a lower absolute energy base.
sections.targets: No metric-specific energy-per-rupee target identified. Relevant targets are Tata’s energy efficiency and renewable procurement, SAIL’s decarbonisation and 384 MW renewable capacity plan, and Jindal’s 50% emissions reduction before FY2035 and net zero by 2050.
sections.comparabilityNotes: This metric is sensitive to steel price, product mix and currency/unit conversions. Jindal FY2023 was reported as 869 GJ/Crore INR and has been converted to GJ/₹ for comparability. SAIL FY2024 was originally disclosed at approximately 1000x the expected level; this appears to be a reporting error rather than a genuine drop in energy efficiency, and has been corrected to 0.00057 GJ/₹ consistent with FY2023.
